title: Efficient Mutation Testing of Quantum Machine Learning Models
abstract: Quantum machine learning integrates the strengths of quantum computing and machine learning, enabling models to learn complex features using fewer parameters than their classical counterparts. Due to the increasing complexity of quantum machine learning models, it is necessary to verify that the implementation of these models satisfy the design specification and be free of bugs and faults. Mutation testing is a promising avenue to identify faulty quantum circuits that do not meet design specifications or contain defects by intentionally inserting faults into the quantum circuit. It is necessary to define mutation operations to inject faults into quantum circuits to ensure that a test suite is robust enough to evaluate an implementation against its design specification. In this paper, we extend mutation testing to quantum machine learning applications, primarily quantum neural network models. Specifically, this paper makes two important contributions. We define new mutation operations for efficient fault insertion compared to state-of-the-art approaches. We also present a directed mutation generation technique to reduce redundant mutant circuits. Extensive experimental evaluation demonstrates that our approach generates a more diverse and representative set of mutants, effectively addressing faults that traditional techniques fail to expose.
summary: This paper introduces an efficient mutation testing framework specifically designed for Quantum Machine Learning models. By targeting the unique structural components of QNNs and QCNNs, the method reduces computational overhead and improves the detection of faults in quantum circuits.
main_problem: Existing mutation testing for quantum circuits is too generalized and inefficient for Quantum Machine Learning (QML) models, leading to excessive redundant mutants and failure to detect application-specific faults.
main_result: The authors developed a directed mutation framework that significantly reduces mutant redundancy (up to 9.5x) while achieving higher mutation scores, particularly through targeted gate and layer modifications.
method: The paper proposes seven new mutation operations (e.g., Ansatz Parameter Change, Layer Shuffling, and Gate Substitution) and a directed generation technique to target the structural components of QNNs and QCNNs.
model_or_system: The study focuses on Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs) and Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNNs) using various feature maps (ZFM, ZZFM, Amplitude Embedding) and ansätze (RealAmplitudes, EfficientSU2).
key_observables: Mutation Score (ratio of killed to total mutants), Survival Rate, and Generation/Evaluation time.
important_parameters: Rotation gate parameters, input data features, and circuit depth (number of layers).
main_assumptions: Assumes that breaking entanglement and symmetry in feature maps is a primary way to induce detectable faults; assumes the use of classical simulators (Qiskit-Aer) for evaluation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the mutation workflow; Figure 2 illustrates standard mutation operations; Figure 3 and 4 detail QNN/feature map structures; Figure 5 shows QCNN architecture; Figure 7 presents the seven new mutation operations; Figure 8 uses t-SNE to visualize decision boundary changes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of QML verification, defines the structural components of QNNs, proposes seven new mutation operations, describes the experimental setup using various datasets and ansätze, and evaluates the efficiency and effectiveness of the new approach against existing methods.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: An overview of mutation testing.

Fig. 2: Example of mutation operations. The CNOT gate between qubits q1 and q2 is removed with a remove gate operation. A new one-qubit gate is added with the add gate operation after the first Hadamard gate on qubit q4. The Pauli-X gate acting on qubit q2 is changed to a semantically equivalent one-qubit gate on the same qubit and in the same location with the change gate operation. The measurement of q2 is removed with the remove measurement operation. A new measurement is added at the end of the circuit on qubit q4 with the add measurement operation. Note that typically one mutation is performed to generate the mutant.

Fig. 5: Typical structure of a quantum convolutional neural network (QCNN). It consists of a convolutional layer followed by a pooling layer. The next round of these pairs of layers reduces the number of qubits it operates over.

Fig. 3: Components of a quantum neural network. The data is first embedded into the quantum circuit using a feature mapping. With the embedded data, the ansatz can process it, learning features and other relationships depending on the model objective. Finally, measurement of the quantum circuit produces the result of the model, such as a class label in classification tasks.
Fig. 4: Example of two-qubit ZZ feature map. x[0] and x[1] are the values taken from the input data sample.
